title: Smash and Grab: Annexation of Sikkim
description: Smash and Grab: Annexation of Sikkim is a book by Sunanda K. Datta-Ray. The book dealt with India's annexation of Sikkim.
descriptionParagraphs: Smash and Grab: Annexation of Sikkim is a book by Sunanda K. Datta-Ray. The book dealt with India's annexation of Sikkim.

The surviving record is interesting because it shows how even ordinary-looking books can acquire a charged political afterlife. As a book, it can be read not only for subject matter but for the way form, tone, and circulation make a text feel dangerous, intimate, or politically usable to anxious officials.

It also matters as part of a wider censorship history in India. The present page is a dossier starter built from source-tracked ban records; the surviving note currently says: The book dealt with India's annexation of Sikkim. The Delhi High Court had stopped its publication after a political officer station in Gangtok at the time filed a defamation suit. The book was later allowed for release. More publication history, translations, and close reading can be added later.

overview.englishDescription: Smash and Grab: Annexation of Sikkim is a book by Sunanda K. Datta-Ray. The book dealt with India's annexation of Sikkim.
banningParagraphs: Smash and Grab: Annexation of Sikkim entered censorship debates as a book associated with controversy, publication history, and state scrutiny. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around political sensitivity.

The earliest event currently captured here is 1984 in India, where Government of India or British Indian authorities banned publication, sale, or possession. The book dealt with India's annexation of Sikkim. The Delhi High Court had stopped its publication after a political officer station in Gangtok at the time filed a defamation suit. The book was later allowed for release. The book dealt with India's annexation of Sikkim. counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
